nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline of northwestern Lake Tanganyika between Luhanga and Makabola; found hugging rock faces and crevices in the shallow littoral zone

decorPreferences: Dense rock piles with caves and crevices for territory and spawning
Flat slate or rock slabs as spawning surfaces
PVC tubes or clay pots as additional cave structures

diet.notes: In the wild feeds on zooplankton and benthic invertebrates gleaned from algal mats on rock surfaces (trophic level 3.5). In aquaria accepts high-quality cichlid micro-pellets, frozen cyclops, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. Spirulina-enriched flake and small sinking wafers are beneficial. Does not typically surface-feed; sinking or near-substrate foods are preferred.

goodWith: Other Tanganyikan cichlids of different genera (Altolamprologus, Neolamprologus, Cyprichromis)
Shell dwellers (Lamprologus ocellatus, Neolamprologus multifasciatus) in larger tanks
avoidWith: Other Julidochromis species (hybridization risk and interspecific aggression)
Julidochromis marlieri (fw-fish-082) — larger, grid-patterned species; easily confused in trade
Unpaired conspecifics introduced to an established pair's territory
Malawi or Victorian cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and aggression profile)
Small, timid fish vulnerable to territorial harassment

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image; verify availability and license before use.

summary: The smallest Julidochromis (~7 cm), endemic to a narrow stretch of northwestern Lake Tanganyika's rocky coast. Sold under several geographic color forms ('Black Widow', 'Bemba', 'Kissi', 'Pemba'). Forms lifelong monogamous pairs; biparental cave spawner. IUCN Critically Endangered due to its extremely restricted range.
careNotes:
Julidochromis transcriptus is the smallest member of its genus, reaching a maximum of approximately 7 cm total length, and is endemic to a very narrow stretch of the northwestern Lake Tanganyika shoreline between Luhanga and Makabola in the Democratic Republic of Congo. Hard, highly alkaline rift-lake chemistry is essential: target pH 8.2–9.0, gH 10–20, kH 8–18. Soft or even moderately hard tap water must be buffered with Tanganyika salt mixes or calcium/magnesium carbonate supplements.

Several distinct geographic color forms are regularly traded: 'Black Widow' (high-contrast black-and-white), 'Bemba', 'Kissi', and 'Pemba'. Each form should be kept pure — do not mix color forms, as they interbreed freely and hybrids lack the clean patterning of wild-type fish.

J. transcriptus is frequently confused in the trade with J. marlieri (fw-fish-082), which is substantially larger (up to 11–15 cm) and has a grid or chequered pattern over much of the body rather than the mask-and-spot pattern of transcriptus. When purchasing, confirm the species by pattern and expected adult size.

The species forms stable, lifelong monogamous pair bonds; once a compatible pair is established, remove all other conspecifics as the pair will fiercely defend territory. Pairs engage in biparental brood care. A minimum 80 L tank with a 60 cm footprint is suitable for a bonded pair alone; 120+ L is recommended if Tanganyikan community tankmates are included. Rockwork should be extensive, providing multiple caves and crevices. This species characteristically swims parallel to rock surfaces and can often be observed hugging vertical or overhanging faces.

Avoid mixing with other Julidochromis species, as hybridization is a well-documented problem in the hobby. Water changes should be gradual (10–15% twice weekly preferred over large weekly changes) to avoid chemistry swings that can disrupt active spawning pairs.

FishBase (2025) lists this species as IUCN Critically Endangered, reflecting its extremely restricted wild distribution rather than any particular husbandry difficulty.
breedingNotes: Breeding is generally straightforward for an established bonded pair and is considered easy once pair formation is complete. Pairs spawn secretively inside caves or flat-rock crevices; the female deposits adhesive eggs on cave walls or rock surfaces, typically 30–80 eggs per clutch. Both parents guard eggs and fry (biparental substrate-spawner). Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 25–26°C; fry become free-swimming after a further 5–7 days and accept freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-pellets from first feeding. Brood care is extended, with fry often remaining in the parents' territory for several weeks. The main challenge is forming a compatible pair: purchasing a group of 4–6 juveniles and allowing natural pairing in a large enough tank, then removing surplus fish once a pair has formed, is the standard approach. Avoid large water changes or rearranging decor once active spawning has begun.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Julidochromis transcriptus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (60–65 mm SL), native range (Luhanga–Makabola, NW Lake Tanganyika, DRC), and habitat (rocky coastal zones). Water chemistry parameters not published on this page at time of access; cross-referenced against FishBase, Wikipedia, and J. marlieri (fw-fish-082) Tanganyika parameters for pH 8.2–9.0 and hardness ranges.

additional.name: FishBase — Julidochromis transcriptus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 7.0 cm TL), temperature (22–25°C; widened to 23–27°C per hobbyist consensus for Tanganyika tanks), pH (8.5–9.2; lowered floor to 8.2 to match practical Tanganyika husbandry), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), diet (zooplankton/benthic invertebrates, trophic level 3.5), native range, and IUCN status (Critically Endangered, March 2025). Minimum aquarium length noted as 60 cm for a pair.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Julidochromis transcriptus
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~7 cm), native range (NW Lake Tanganyika), habitat preference (rocky substrates), diet (zooplankton and benthic invertebrates from algal mats), and IUCN Critically Endangered status.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Julidochromis marlieri (fw-fish-082 reference)
additional.notes: Used as a cross-genus Tanganyika parameter anchor for water chemistry (pH 7.8–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 8–20, temperatureC 23–27). Marlieri profile also confirmed the confusable-species relationship, grid vs. mask pattern distinction, and hybridization risk across the Julidochromis genus.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental cave/substrate spawner; 30–80 adhesive eggs per clutch deposited on cave walls or rock surfaces; hatches ~48–72 h at 25°C; free-swimming fry accept BBS nauplii from day 1. Main challenge is forming a compatible pair — start with 4–6 juveniles, allow natural pairing, remove extras.
